title: The catch block in the request handler swallows all errors indiscriminately, treating permission errors, out-of-memory, and ENOENT identically as 404.
description: The `try { const data = await readFile(fsPath); ... } catch { return new Response('not found: ...', { status: 404 }); }` catches any error thrown by `readFile`. An `ENOENT` (file not found) is correctly mapped to 404, but `EACCES` (permission denied), `EISDIR` (path is a directory), or an OOM error would all silently become 404 responses with a misleading body. For a dev server this is unlikely to cause confusion, but a developer wondering why a file isn't being served might be misled into thinking the path is wrong rather than that there's a permission issue.

title: page.evaluate accesses `c._meta` without checking that `c` is non-null, relying on the JS ternary — but the returned `meta` is never validated before logging.
description: The `page.evaluate` callback returns `c ? c._meta : null`. If `c` is found but `_meta` is undefined (e.g., the property was never set because processing failed differently than expected), `meta` will be `undefined` serialised as `null` by the structured-clone algorithm, and `console.log('meta:', meta)` will print `meta: null` with no indication of why. The script provides no fallback diagnostic for this case.

title: TIFF header magic check and all subsequent offset reads have no bounds checks; a truncated or corrupt file will produce uncaught RangeError or silent garbage reads.
description: The script reads `data[0]`, `data[1]`, then calls `u32(4)` to get `ifd0_off`. None of these check that the buffer is large enough. For a file shorter than 8 bytes, `u32(4)` will read `undefined` values and return `NaN` or 0 due to JavaScript's behaviour with out-of-bounds typed array access (returns `undefined`, which when ORed/shifted becomes 0). Subsequent reads at offset 0 would then walk IFD0 from the start of the file, producing confusing output. A bounds check at the top (`if (data.length < 8) { console.error('File too small'); process.exit(1); }`) would catch corrupt inputs early.

title: readFileSync for the ORF file has no try/catch; if the file does not exist the process exits with an undecorated Node ENOENT stack trace.
description: All five scripts use synchronous `readFileSync` calls without wrapping them in try/catch. When the default path does not exist on the machine running the script, the error message from Node/Bun will be a raw ENOENT with a stack trace rather than a helpful usage message. For a dev diagnostic script this is minor, but a simple `if (!existsSync(ORF_PATH))` guard with a clear message would improve the experience.

title: sharp().toFile() rejection is not caught; if the output PNG path is not writable the process will exit with an unhandled rejection.
description: The `await sharp(...).toFile(OUT)` call at the bottom of `compare.ts` is inside a top-level await context so any rejection will propagate as an uncaught top-level error and terminate the process. While the error will be printed, it won't include context about which step failed or what the output path was. Since `OUT` is a hardcoded absolute path to a specific machine, this is a common failure point for other developers.
